title: 🎙️ Working Lands & Industry
description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Working Lands & Industry' — the resource economy that defines the Cariboo and rural BC.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Trade-press journalism about forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture should score well even when it's equipment-, market-, or operations-focused rather than policy-focused — a story about a piece of farm machinery or a mining operation update is a real fit, not a 0:
- 80-100: BC/Cariboo-specific forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture stories (mill closures, beetle kill, BCTS, Cariboo ranchers and water rights, local mine operations, Cariboo Cattlemen events).
- 55-79: Canadian resource-economy trade journalism without a BC tie — Western Producer or Northern Miner-style coverage of farm equipment and machinery, harvest technology, livestock markets, mining operations and exploration, AgTech, forestry practices, sawmills, or labour/commodity stories in the resource sector.
- 30-54: Broader agriculture, forestry, ranching, or mining news (e.g. international resource-industry stories, general farm economics, climate impacts on working lands) with clear working-lands relevance but no Canadian/BC tie.
- 15-29: Tangential resource-sector mentions (e.g. a general business, policy, or science story that touches briefly on forestry/agriculture/mining) or local Cariboo civic news unrelated to working lands.
- 0-14: No connection to forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture.

Strong fits include: BC and Canadian forestry journalism (mill closures, lumber markets, beetle kill, harvest operations, silviculture, BCTS, old-growth policy), ranching and livestock stories (water rights, grazing management, cattle markets, drought impact on ranches), mining and resource extraction (safety, new operations, Indigenous consultation, supply chains, exploration updates), agriculture in rural Canada (crop insurance, grain prices, farm economics, food sovereignty, farm equipment and machinery), and the technology helping these industries adapt — precision forestry, AgTech, drone surveys, IoT in agriculture, mine automation, sawmill modernization, climate adaptation on working lands. Also strong fits: labor and workforce stories in resource industries, environmental stewardship within working landscapes (not pure conservation, but how ranchers/loggers manage for sustainability), and science with direct implications for forestry, agriculture, or extractive industries. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): international geopolitical and war news with no resource-economy connection; consumer tech and gadget reviews; pure wildlife entertainment without agricultural or working-lands relevance; crime and judicial news unrelated to resource industries; financial market and stock ticker stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

🌾 ✏️

An appeals court on Friday upheld Harvey Weinstein's 2022 rape and sexual assault conviction in California, but ordered his trial judge to resentence him.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] Outrage as woman jailed for three years after criticising Somali government online
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Sentencing of 27-year-old Sadia Moalim Ali condemned by former president and prime ministers as well as rights groups

A rickshaw driver in Somalia has been sentenced to three years in prison for comments she made on social media, in a case that has caught the public’s attention and provoked outrage in the country.

Sadia Moalim Ali, a 27-year-old nursing graduate, was originally charged with insulting government institutions and incitement to commit a crime, but convicted only of the former. Her sentence, immediately condemned as “fundamentally unjust”, was handed down on 25 June.

Continue reading...

items.title: [Boing Boing] In 1858 the Thames smelled so bad that Parliament fled and Disraeli called it a 'Stygian pool'
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

By the summer of 1858, three million Londoners were flushing their sewage into the Thames through an aging system that emptied directly into the river. Temperatures hit 48 °C (118 °F) in the sun. The water level dropped. Raw effluent sat on the banks. — Read the rest

The post In 1858 the Thames smelled so bad that Parliament fled and Disraeli called it a 'Stygian pool' appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Kevin Mitnick left the man who helped jail him enough for a Porsche 911
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Kevin Mitnick, the legendary hacker, once tried to social-engineer his way into Novell's network by phoning a network admin named Shawn Nunley late at night, posing as an employee who needed "direct inbound modem access" for a top-secret project.

Nunley smelled a rat, played along, and asked the caller to leave a voicemail — which he recorded onto a cassette recorder. — Read the rest

The post Kevin Mitnick left the man who helped jail him enough for a Porsche 911 appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

House Oversight's Epstein probe took a turn Friday when Chairman James Comer issued two subpoenas to billionaire Leon Black during questioning, after lawmakers said Black refused to answer questions about nondisclosure agreements.

This is the Epstein universe doing what it always does: important men, enormous sums of money, sealed agreements, lawyers saying everything is misunderstood, and Congress trying to pry open the drawer marked "please do not open." — Read the rest

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Convent of Santa Maria de Mave in Santa María de Mave, Spain
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

 This building is in the village of Santa Maria  de Mave ,  The village takes  name from the church and convento at its centre and is close to the larger settlement of Mave close to the border of  Cantabria and Castile y Leòn .

In Spain the term  Convento is used to distinguish the institution  as housing a group of religious members of an order which is active in the community  in which it is based as opposed to a contemplative, isolated  order in which case the building would be termed a monestery. To a certain  extent the terms are interchangeable  and the term "convent" certainly  does not imply that the residents were nuns. In this case the occupants were male friars. The term Monastario is often applied nowadays to the church building directly attached to the Convento with the Romanesque church being in its original religious form (with with a triple apse and three naves,  a bell wall, currently with resident white storks, and a tall dome over the transept). The Convento has been converted from the cloister (rebuilt in the 18th century) to a boutique hotel with a restaurant  and a bar/cafeteria. The conversion  has been done sympathetically with many original feature retained but the architect clearly has a love for the modern. Some of the bedrooms are actually in the monk's cells.

The origins of the Convento and the associated church date as far back as the second half of the 9th century. The abbey was founded by King Alphonse III . In 1033, Mave and its possessions became part of the important Benedictine monastery of San Salvador de Oña, in Burgos. Nothing remains of the ancient Pre-Romanesque structure.
items.summary: This building is in the village of Santa Maria de Mave , The village takes name from the church and convento at its centre and is close to the larger settlement of Mave close to the border of Cantabria and Castile y Leòn . In Spain the term Convento is used to distinguish the institution as housing

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Astronomers found two rare super puff planets lighter than cotton candy
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Two newly confirmed "super-puff" planets are so diffuse that they are less dense than cotton candy, despite being about the size of Jupiter. Their rare orbital relationship and enormous, lightweight atmospheres could provide valuable clues about how some of the strangest planets in the galaxy come to exist.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] ‘It’s open green space versus construction’: Delhi’s historic polo club and the battle for the city’s future
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

The government’s requisition of a historic green space has ignited a fierce debate about air quality and heat stress in India’s scorching capital

For decades, the social highlight of winters in Delhi for the “beautiful people” was the polo season. A sprinkling of royalty and diplomats, impeccably groomed women in pearls and chiffon saris, along with wealthy industrialists sporting silk pocket squares used to gather to watch polo players compete under the mild, balmy sun.

They cheered on handsome players who, once the match was over, had children shrieking in delight as they put on a heart-stopping display of tent-pegging derring-do. Swish champagne lunches and other après-polo celebrations followed.

Continue reading...

items.title: [Gizmodo] Yet Another Piece of AI-Pilled Speculative Fiction Has Gone Dangerously Viral
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Speculative writing starts from some apocalyptic ending, and then the writer works backwards. This is because fiction is a cheat. You toss out reality, and only write the steps it would take to get to your destination. You can be completely upfront about what you’re doing, but readers will ...
